name: Sudan war (RSF vs SAF, 2023-)
summary: The Sudan war began on 15 April 2023 between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF). Reported fatalities reached at least 59,000 by April 2026, but mortality studies and humanitarian estimates suggest the true toll from violence, hunger, and health-system collapse is much higher, with published estimates ranging from about 150,000 to 400,000. Civilian deaths are heavily undercounted, and most combatant and indirect deaths cannot be reliably attributed to one side.

sides.name: Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and allied forces

military_killed.notes: No independent nationwide SAF military-death total was found. ACLED reports battle fatalities but does not split them by SAF vs RSF. A localized SAF-linked claim reported 8,600 6th Infantry Division personnel killed in El Fasher by August 2025, but this is not a nationwide verified figure.

breakdown.notes: ACLED attributed 2,083 civilian-targeting fatalities to state forces from 15 April 2023 to 31 December 2024. This is a minimum reported count and excludes many unreported and later deaths.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians in residential areas, markets, schools, hospitals, and displacement camps
breakdown.notes: Sudan Witness/CIR reported at least 1,719 civilians killed by SAF airstrikes from April 2023 to July 2025. This likely overlaps with other civilian-targeting datasets and is not added separately to the total low.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians at Kabkabiya market, North Darfur
breakdown.notes: OHCHR-linked reporting cited at least 42 civilians killed in the 9 December 2024 Kabkabiya market strike; other rights/media reports cited over 100 deaths.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians at Tora market, North Darfur
breakdown.notes: AP reported at least 54 killed in a March 2025 military airstrike on a local market; UN and rights groups also reported claims of hundreds killed, but no reliable high figure is used here.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: SAF/state-force direct civilian killings are best documented through ACLED state-force civilian targeting and independent airstrike investigations. Totals are minimum reported figures and should not be added mechanically because datasets overlap.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Sudanese civilians affected by destruction of health, water, power, food, and market infrastructure
deaths_from_actions.notes: The UN Fact-Finding Mission found both SAF and RSF responsible for large-scale attacks on civilians and destruction of infrastructure essential to survival, but no SAF-only indirect-death estimate is available.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians in Khartoum State and other conflict-affected areas
deaths_from_actions.notes: LSHTM estimated 61,202 all-cause deaths in Khartoum State in the first 14 months, including 26,024 intentional-injury deaths; the difference includes preventable disease, hunger, accidents, and health-care collapse, but cannot be attributed to SAF alone.

sides.name: Rapid Support Forces (RSF), allied Arab militias, and aligned forces
members: RSF-aligned local militias and armed groups

military_killed.notes: No independent nationwide RSF combatant-death total was found. ACLED battle fatalities and total reported fatalities are not split into RSF vs SAF combatants.

breakdown.notes: ACLED attributed 5,305 civilian-targeting fatalities to rebel groups from 15 April 2023 to 31 December 2024, primarily RSF and aligned forces in this conflict. This is a minimum reported count and excludes many unreported atrocities and later events.

breakdown.victims_group: Massalit and other non-Arab civilians in El Geneina, West Darfur
breakdown.notes: UN Panel of Experts estimate reported by Reuters; HRW documented RSF and allied militia attacks as ethnic cleansing and crimes against humanity.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians and fleeing civilians in El Fasher, North Darfur
breakdown.notes: OHCHR documented more than 6,000 killings between 25 and 27 October 2025, including at least 4,400 inside El Fasher and more than 1,600 along exit routes. The UN stated the actual toll for the week-long offensive was higher.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians in Zamzam IDP camp, North Darfur
breakdown.notes: The UN reported more than 1,000 civilians killed in a three-day RSF attack on Zamzam camp in April 2025.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians in eastern and northern Al Jazirah/Gezira State
breakdown.notes: EUAA cited estimates of more than 1,237 civilians killed in RSF attacks between 20 October and 11 November 2024.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians in Wad al-Noura, Gezira State
breakdown.notes: AP reported at least 100 people killed in RSF attacks on Wad al-Noura on 5 June 2024; UNICEF said at least 35 children were killed.
AP reported 54 killed and at least 158 wounded in a 1 February 2025 attack by paramilitary forces.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The low value is rounded and represents a conservative roll-up of major documented RSF-attributed events, not a comprehensive RSF civilian-death total. It excludes many smaller attacks, unreported killings, and deaths from hunger, disease, or displacement.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians in besieged Darfur and Kordofan areas, including El Fasher and displacement camps
deaths_from_actions.notes: The UN Fact-Finding Mission found that RSF and allied militias used starvation as a method of warfare and deprived civilians of food, medicine, and relief supplies. No RSF-only indirect-death total is available.

WFP and IPC-related reporting confirmed famine conditions in conflict-cut-off areas and warned of severe malnutrition and mortality risk. Attribution to RSF alone is not possible because SAF actions, RSF actions, access restrictions, market collapse, and displacement all interact.

totals.notes: Military deaths by side are unknown. The civilian_low of 8,194 is ACLED's reported civilian-targeting fatality total through 31 December 2024 and is not a full civilian death toll. The grand_low of 59,000 is ACLED's reported-fatality floor by April 2026. The grand_high of 400,000 reflects the high end of published all-cause estimates summarized by CSIS; 150,000 is a commonly cited lower all-cause estimate.

key_atrocities.name: El Geneina / West Darfur ethnic-cleansing campaign
key_atrocities.notes: UN Panel of Experts estimate reported by Reuters; HRW documented ethnic cleansing and crimes against humanity.

key_atrocities.name: El Fasher final offensive and massacre
key_atrocities.victims_group: Civilians and fleeing civilians in El Fasher
key_atrocities.notes: OHCHR documented more than 6,000 killings in the first three days, including at least 4,400 inside the city and more than 1,600 along exit routes.

key_atrocities.name: Zamzam IDP camp attack
key_atrocities.notes: UN reported more than 1,000 civilians killed in a three-day RSF attack.

key_atrocities.name: Eastern and northern Gezira massacres
key_atrocities.notes: EUAA cited reporting of more than 1,237 civilians killed between 20 October and 11 November 2024.

key_atrocities.name: Wad al-Noura massacre
key_atrocities.notes: AP reported at least 100 killed; UNICEF said at least 35 children were killed.

key_atrocities.name: Kabkabiya market airstrike
key_atrocities.notes: OHCHR-linked reporting cited at least 42 civilians killed; other rights/media reports cited over 100 deaths.

key_atrocities.name: Omdurman Sabrein market attack
key_atrocities.notes: AP reported 54 killed and at least 158 wounded.

uncertainty_notes: The largest uncertainty is the lack of independent nationwide military casualty counts and the absence of perpetrator-attributed indirect mortality estimates. ACLED is a reported-fatality dataset and severely undercounts inaccessible areas; LSHTM's mortality work demonstrates that Khartoum State alone likely had more all-cause deaths in 14 months than many national reported-death counts. Direct civilian killings are documented best for major RSF atrocities and for SAF airstrikes, but these are still minimum figures and often overlap across datasets.

sources.title: The Sudan War in 10 Charts
